[[ja: 金幣社]] {{wikidata link|Q119929592}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:金幣社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:金幣社]] {{Expand Japanese|金幣社|date=November 2025}} <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:金幣社]]
{{nihongo|'''Kinpei-sha'''|金幣社}} is a shrine ranking in Gifu prefecture
“Kinpeisha” (きんぺいしゃ) is a shrine ranking system (shakaku) independently implemented by the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines in Gifu Prefecture after World War II. Its official name is “Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines Director’s Envoy–Designated Shrine.” Lower ranks include “Ginpeisha” (ぎんぺいしゃ; Branch Director’s Envoy–Designated Shrine) and “Hakuheisha” (はくへいしゃ; Section Chair’s Envoy–Designated Shrine), which are also explained in this article.
== Overview == On February 2, 1946 (Shōwa 21), following the end of the Pacific War, the GHQ (Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers) issued the Shinto Directive, abolishing state control of shrines and, at the same time, the modern shrine ranking system. As a result, contributions became uniform regardless of a shrine’s size, leading to inequities. To address this, in 1952 (Shōwa 27) the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines began designating shrines to receive offerings from an official envoy.
Under the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines’ “Regulations Concerning the Dispatch of Offering Envoys,” three ranks are defined: Kinpeisha, Ginpeisha, and Hakuheisha. Former prefectural shrines were designated Kinpeisha, former township shrines Ginpeisha, and former village shrines Hakuheisha. A Kinpeisha is a shrine visited by the Director of the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, who presents golden ritual offerings (kinpei); a Ginpeisha is visited by a branch director who presents silver offerings (ginpei); and a Hakuheisha is visited by a section chair who presents white offerings (hakubei). Designated shrines receive regular visits from offering envoys.
When the system began in 1952 (Shōwa 27), there were 16 Kinpeisha, 51 Ginpeisha, and 15 Hakuheisha. Thereafter, the number of designated shrines increased; by 1991 (Heisei 3), there were 88 Kinpeisha, 252 Ginpeisha, and 351 Hakuheisha. As of 2021 (Reiwa 3), the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines oversaw approximately 3,200 shrines, of which about 90 were Kinpeisha. As of 2025 (Reiwa 7), there are 89 Kinpeisha.
In some branches of the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, stamp rallies are held that allow participants to visit Kinpeisha within the city.
In addition, some shrines—such as Keta Wakamiya Shrine, Hida Sōsha, and Ōtsu Shrine (Hida City)—are ranked as “Special Kinpeisha.”
[[ja: 金幣社]] {{wikidata link|Q119929592}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:金幣社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:金幣社]] {{Expand Japanese|金幣社|date=November 2025}} <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:金幣社]]
{{nihongo|'''Kinpei-sha'''|金幣社}} is a shrine ranking in Gifu prefecture
“Kinpeisha” (きんぺいしゃ) is a shrine ranking system (shakaku) independently implemented by the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines in Gifu Prefecture after World War II. Its official name is “Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines Director’s Envoy–Designated Shrine.” Lower ranks include “Ginpeisha” (ぎんぺいしゃ; Branch Director’s Envoy–Designated Shrine) and “Hakuheisha” (はくへいしゃ; Section Chair’s Envoy–Designated Shrine), which are also explained in this article.
== Overview == On February 2, 1946 (Shōwa 21), following the end of the Pacific War, the GHQ (Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers) issued the Shinto Directive, abolishing state control of shrines and, at the same time, the modern shrine ranking system. As a result, contributions became uniform regardless of a shrine’s size, leading to inequities. To address this, in 1952 (Shōwa 27) the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines began designating shrines to receive offerings from an official envoy.
Under the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines’ “Regulations Concerning the Dispatch of Offering Envoys,” three ranks are defined: Kinpeisha, Ginpeisha, and Hakuheisha. Former prefectural shrines were designated Kinpeisha, former township shrines Ginpeisha, and former village shrines Hakuheisha. A Kinpeisha is a shrine visited by the Director of the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, who presents golden ritual offerings (kinpei); a Ginpeisha is visited by a branch director who presents silver offerings (ginpei); and a Hakuheisha is visited by a section chair who presents white offerings (hakubei). Designated shrines receive regular visits from offering envoys.
When the system began in 1952 (Shōwa 27), there were 16 Kinpeisha, 51 Ginpeisha, and 15 Hakuheisha. Thereafter, the number of designated shrines increased; by 1991 (Heisei 3), there were 88 Kinpeisha, 252 Ginpeisha, and 351 Hakuheisha. As of 2021 (Reiwa 3), the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines oversaw approximately 3,200 shrines, of which about 90 were Kinpeisha. As of 2025 (Reiwa 7), there are 89 Kinpeisha.
In some branches of the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, stamp rallies are held that allow participants to visit Kinpeisha within the city.
In addition, some shrines—such as [[Keta Wakamiya Shrine]], [[Hida Sōja]], and [[Ōtsu Shrine (Hida)]]—are ranked as “Special Kinpeisha.”
[[ja: 金幣社]] {{wikidata link|Q119929592}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:金幣社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:金幣社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:金幣社]]
{{nihongo|'''Kinpei-sha'''|金幣社}} is a shrine ranking in Gifu prefecture
“Kinpeisha” (きんぺいしゃ) is a shrine ranking system (shakaku) independently implemented by the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines in Gifu Prefecture after World War II. Its official name is “Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines Director’s Envoy–Designated Shrine.” Lower ranks include “Ginpeisha” (ぎんぺいしゃ; Branch Director’s Envoy–Designated Shrine) and “Hakuheisha” (はくへいしゃ; Section Chair’s Envoy–Designated Shrine), which are also explained in this article.
== Overview == On February 2, 1946 (Shōwa 21), following the end of the Pacific War, the GHQ (Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers) issued the Shinto Directive, abolishing state control of shrines and, at the same time, the modern shrine ranking system. As a result, contributions became uniform regardless of a shrine’s size, leading to inequities. To address this, in 1952 (Shōwa 27) the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines began designating shrines to receive offerings from an official envoy.
Under the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines’ “Regulations Concerning the Dispatch of Offering Envoys,” three ranks are defined: Kinpeisha, Ginpeisha, and Hakuheisha. Former prefectural shrines were designated Kinpeisha, former township shrines Ginpeisha, and former village shrines Hakuheisha. A Kinpeisha is a shrine visited by the Director of the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, who presents golden ritual offerings (kinpei); a Ginpeisha is visited by a branch director who presents silver offerings (ginpei); and a Hakuheisha is visited by a section chair who presents white offerings (hakubei). Designated shrines receive regular visits from offering envoys.
When the system began in 1952 (Shōwa 27), there were 16 Kinpeisha, 51 Ginpeisha, and 15 Hakuheisha. Thereafter, the number of designated shrines increased; by 1991 (Heisei 3), there were 88 Kinpeisha, 252 Ginpeisha, and 351 Hakuheisha. As of 2021 (Reiwa 3), the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines oversaw approximately 3,200 shrines, of which about 90 were Kinpeisha. As of 2025 (Reiwa 7), there are 89 Kinpeisha.
In some branches of the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, stamp rallies are held that allow participants to visit Kinpeisha within the city.
In addition, some shrines—such as [[Keta Wakamiya Shrine]], [[Hida Sōja]], and [[Ōtsu Shrine (Hida)]]—are ranked as “Special Kinpeisha.”
[[ja: 金幣社]] {{wikidata link|Q119929592}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

{{nihongo|'''Kinpei-sha'''|金幣社}} is a shrine ranking in Gifu prefecture
“Kinpeisha” (きんぺいしゃ) is a shrine ranking system (shakaku) independently implemented by the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines in Gifu Prefecture after World War II. Its official name is “Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines Director’s Envoy–Designated Shrine.” Lower ranks include “Ginpeisha” (ぎんぺいしゃ; Branch Director’s Envoy–Designated Shrine) and “Hakuheisha” (はくへいしゃ; Section Chair’s Envoy–Designated Shrine), which are also explained in this article.
== Overview == On February 2, 1946 (Shōwa 21), following the end of the Pacific War, the GHQ (Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers) issued the Shinto Directive, abolishing state control of shrines and, at the same time, the modern shrine ranking system. As a result, contributions became uniform regardless of a shrine’s size, leading to inequities. To address this, in 1952 (Shōwa 27) the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines began designating shrines to receive offerings from an official envoy.
Under the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines’ “Regulations Concerning the Dispatch of Offering Envoys,” three ranks are defined: Kinpeisha, Ginpeisha, and Hakuheisha. Former prefectural shrines were designated Kinpeisha, former township shrines Ginpeisha, and former village shrines Hakuheisha. A Kinpeisha is a shrine visited by the Director of the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, who presents golden ritual offerings (kinpei); a Ginpeisha is visited by a branch director who presents silver offerings (ginpei); and a Hakuheisha is visited by a section chair who presents white offerings (hakubei). Designated shrines receive regular visits from offering envoys.
When the system began in 1952 (Shōwa 27), there were 16 Kinpeisha, 51 Ginpeisha, and 15 Hakuheisha. Thereafter, the number of designated shrines increased; by 1991 (Heisei 3), there were 88 Kinpeisha, 252 Ginpeisha, and 351 Hakuheisha. As of 2021 (Reiwa 3), the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines oversaw approximately 3,200 shrines, of which about 90 were Kinpeisha. As of 2025 (Reiwa 7), there are 89 Kinpeisha.
In some branches of the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, stamp rallies are held that allow participants to visit Kinpeisha within the city.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:金幣社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:金幣社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:金幣社]]
{{nihongo|'''Kinpei-sha'''|金幣社}} is a shrine ranking in Gifu prefecture
“Kinpeisha” (きんぺいしゃ) is a shrine ranking system (shakaku) independently implemented by the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines in Gifu Prefecture after World War II. Its official name is “Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines Director’s Envoy–Designated Shrine.” Lower ranks include “Ginpeisha” (ぎんぺいしゃ; Branch Director’s Envoy–Designated Shrine) and “Hakuheisha” (はくへいしゃ; Section Chair’s Envoy–Designated Shrine), which are also explained in this article.
== Overview == On February 2, 1946 (Shōwa 21), following the end of the Pacific War, the GHQ (Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers) issued the Shinto Directive, abolishing state control of shrines and, at the same time, the modern shrine ranking system. As a result, contributions became uniform regardless of a shrine’s size, leading to inequities. To address this, in 1952 (Shōwa 27) the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines began designating shrines to receive offerings from an official envoy.
Under the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines’ “Regulations Concerning the Dispatch of Offering Envoys,” three ranks are defined: Kinpeisha, Ginpeisha, and Hakuheisha. Former prefectural shrines were designated Kinpeisha, former township shrines Ginpeisha, and former village shrines Hakuheisha. A Kinpeisha is a shrine visited by the Director of the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, who presents golden ritual offerings (kinpei); a Ginpeisha is visited by a branch director who presents silver offerings (ginpei); and a Hakuheisha is visited by a section chair who presents white offerings (hakubei). Designated shrines receive regular visits from offering envoys.
When the system began in 1952 (Shōwa 27), there were 16 Kinpeisha, 51 Ginpeisha, and 15 Hakuheisha. Thereafter, the number of designated shrines increased; by 1991 (Heisei 3), there were 88 Kinpeisha, 252 Ginpeisha, and 351 Hakuheisha. As of 2021 (Reiwa 3), the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines oversaw approximately 3,200 shrines, of which about 90 were Kinpeisha. As of 2025 (Reiwa 7), there are 89 Kinpeisha.
In some branches of the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, stamp rallies are held that allow participants to visit Kinpeisha within the city.
In addition, some shrines—such as [[Keta Wakamiya Shrine]], [[Hida Sōja]], and [[Ōtsu Shrine (Hida)]]—are ranked as “Special Kinpeisha.”
[[ja: 金幣社]] {{wikidata link|Q119929592}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.comment: Bot: strip HTML comments; consolidate 4 interlanguage link(s) into wikidata link template [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/25089550702|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kinpei-sha'''|金幣社}} is a shrine ranking in Gifu prefecture
“Kinpeisha” (きんぺいしゃ) is a shrine ranking system (shakaku) independently implemented by the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines in Gifu Prefecture after World War II. Its official name is “Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines Director’s Envoy–Designated Shrine.” Lower ranks include “Ginpeisha” (ぎんぺいしゃ; Branch Director’s Envoy–Designated Shrine) and “Hakuheisha” (はくへいしゃ; Section Chair’s Envoy–Designated Shrine), which are also explained in this article.
== Overview == On February 2, 1946 (Shōwa 21), following the end of the Pacific War, the GHQ (Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers) issued the Shinto Directive, abolishing state control of shrines and, at the same time, the modern shrine ranking system. As a result, contributions became uniform regardless of a shrine’s size, leading to inequities. To address this, in 1952 (Shōwa 27) the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines began designating shrines to receive offerings from an official envoy.
Under the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines’ “Regulations Concerning the Dispatch of Offering Envoys,” three ranks are defined: Kinpeisha, Ginpeisha, and Hakuheisha. Former prefectural shrines were designated Kinpeisha, former township shrines Ginpeisha, and former village shrines Hakuheisha. A Kinpeisha is a shrine visited by the Director of the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, who presents golden ritual offerings (kinpei); a Ginpeisha is visited by a branch director who presents silver offerings (ginpei); and a Hakuheisha is visited by a section chair who presents white offerings (hakubei). Designated shrines receive regular visits from offering envoys.
When the system began in 1952 (Shōwa 27), there were 16 Kinpeisha, 51 Ginpeisha, and 15 Hakuheisha. Thereafter, the number of designated shrines increased; by 1991 (Heisei 3), there were 88 Kinpeisha, 252 Ginpeisha, and 351 Hakuheisha. As of 2021 (Reiwa 3), the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines oversaw approximately 3,200 shrines, of which about 90 were Kinpeisha. As of 2025 (Reiwa 7), there are 89 Kinpeisha.
In some branches of the Gifu Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, stamp rallies are held that allow participants to visit Kinpeisha within the city.
In addition, some shrines—such as [[Keta Wakamiya Shrine]], [[Hida Sōja]], and [[Ōtsu Shrine (Hida)]]—are ranked as “Special Kinpeisha.”
{{wikidata link|Q119929592|ja|金幣社}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
